topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a lead weight. *Party cancelled.* I stared at the screen, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. Sarah’s carefully curated excitement, the planning, the sheer volume of effort—all vaporized by a single, sterile notification. I didn't move for a full minute, just letting the silence of my apartment amplify the sting. Then, I slammed the laptop shut. The sound echoed sharply in the quiet room. I stood up, pacing the worn rug, my hands flexing at my sides. This wasn't over. I pulled out my phone, not to check social media, but to draft a reply. It wouldn't be a polite acknowledgment; it would be a declaration. I typed quickly, the keys clicking with sharp, impatient rhythm. "Fine," I muttered to the empty room, deleting the draft, and then starting over, injecting every ounce of my frustration into the next few lines.

[story 2]

When Mark told me, his voice flat and devoid of the usual buoyant energy, the air in the room seemed to thin out. I felt a strange, hot pressure building behind my sternum. I didn't cry. Instead, I met his gaze, holding it steady until he seemed to falter. "Cancelled?" I repeated, the word tasting like ash. I crossed my arms tightly over my chest, a physical barrier against the sudden wave of resentment washing over me. I took a deep, deliberate breath, letting the air fill my lungs until I felt undeniably full. "That's... fine," I managed, my voice surprisingly level, though the edge of it was razor-sharp. I didn't look away. I simply waited, letting the implication hang heavy between us—that this disappointment was entirely his to manage, and mine was simply a fact to be processed, not a tragedy to be mourned.

[story 3]

The news hit me mid-laugh, a sharp, unexpected jolt that made my whole body tense up. I felt a surge of heat rise up my neck, a stubborn refusal to accept the deflation. I walked over to the window, planting my palms against the cool glass, staring out at the indifferent city lights. I didn't shout; shouting felt too messy, too yielding. Instead, I slowly turned back toward the living room, my posture rigid. I picked up the discarded decorations—a stray balloon, a crumpled banner—and deliberately smoothed them out, examining the remnants of the planned joy with cold scrutiny. I tossed the items back onto the table with a deliberate lack of care. "Whatever," I said, the words clipped and brittle. I didn't wait for a response. I turned my back on the source of the news, walking toward the kitchen, already planning how to make something entirely new, something that wouldn't require anyone else's permission.
